fields.description:
## Why

The audit UI needs an optional, disabled-by-default, read-only local web surface that is fully inert
when off. Establishing that surface — the packaging extra, the config flag (with ALL its wiring), lazy
imports, the loopback binding, and a running server that renders one real page end-to-end — is the
riskiest architectural piece (gating + packaging + binding), so it ships first as a thin vertical
slice that proves the whole optional-surface mechanism.

## What

A `rebar audit serve` subcommand that, when enabled, starts a FastAPI server (reusing the `reviewbot`
web stack) bound to 127.0.0.1, serving a read-only INDEX page listing tickets that have audit data
(each linking to a per-ticket page that the sibling rendering story fills in). Gated by a new
default-false `[ui] enabled` config flag and a new `nava-rebar[ui]` optional extra; all web imports
are lazy so disabled / absent-extra = inert. Default port `8765` (overridable with `--port`).

## Dependencies (the read layer — ALREADY LANDED on `main`)

This story depends on the audit read layer delivered by the data-enrichment epic's story E
(`rebar.audit.read`), which is **already merged on `main`**. Pinned contract this story builds on:

- `rebar.audit.read.audit_trail(ticket_id: str, *, repo_root=None) -> dict` — best-effort, NEVER
  raises. Returns an `AuditTrail` dict:
  `{"ticket": dict, "plan_reviews": list[dict] (newest-first), "completion": {"attestation": dict|None,
  "sidecar": dict|None} | None, "code_reviews": list[dict] ({"ticket_id", "sidecars": list[dict]})}`.
- There is **no** `list_audited_tickets()` helper in the read layer. The index route therefore
  **enumerates audited tickets itself**: iterate `rebar.list_tickets()` (id + type) and include a
  ticket when its `audit_trail(id)` shows any audit data — i.e. non-empty `plan_reviews`, a non-`None`
  `completion`, or non-empty `code_reviews`. This enumeration lives in `server.py` (a small
  `_audited_ticket_ids()` / index helper), not in the read layer (which this story does not modify).

Because the read layer is landed, the live end-to-end AC is reachable **within this story alone** (no
stub needed): the E2E test seeds a ticket with a plan-review sidecar and asserts it appears on the
index.

## Endpoint access contract (human/admin auth)

The server binds 127.0.0.1 (loopback) ONLY and serves READ-ONLY content; there is no state-changing
endpoint. Human access control is the loopback boundary — no network principal can reach it — the
justified no-auth posture (matching datasette / mlflow / `git instaweb --local`). Binding a
non-loopback host requires an explicit `--host` flag that prints a warning to stderr; actually
exposing the service to a network (and any auth then required) is OUT of scope for this epic —
documented, not implemented. No machine credentials are involved.

## Scope

- `pyproject.toml` — add `[project.optional-dependencies] ui = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]",
  "jinja2"]` (reusing the reviewbot stack); document the extra with a comment matching the neighbouring
  `reviewbot`/`agents` extras.
- `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` — the FULL `UiConfig` wiring (a partial edit is silently dropped as an
  unknown section by `coerce_sparse`): (1) add a `UiConfig` dataclass with `enabled: bool = False`;
  (2) add `ui: UiConfig = field(default_factory=UiConfig)` to the top-level `Config` dataclass;
  (3) register `"ui": UiConfig` in `_SECTION_CLASSES`; (4) add `"ui": {"enabled": lambda v, k:
  _as_bool(v, k)}` to the `_SECTIONS` coercer map — yielding env override `REBAR_UI_ENABLED`.
- `src/rebar/config.py` — add an **explicit** per-class re-export line `from rebar._config_schema import
  UiConfig as UiConfig` (this module re-exports each `_config_schema` dataclass with an explicit
  `... as ...` line — lines 30–40; it does NOT wildcard-export, so a new class must be added by hand);
  document the key in `docs/config.md`.
- New `src/rebar/audit/server.py` (< 500 LOC) — the FastAPI app + a `serve(host, port)` entry,
  lazy-imported inside the CLI handler with a friendly "install nava-rebar[ui]" `SystemExit` on
  `ModuleNotFoundError`; binds 127.0.0.1 by default; one index route enumerating audited tickets (see
  Dependencies) and linking each to its per-ticket page. All `fastapi`/`jinja2` imports live inside
  `server.py` (module scope is fine — nothing in core imports `server.py`, and `import rebar` /
  `import rebar.audit` never pull it, keeping the disabled/absent-extra path inert).
- CLI — `audit` is **already** an intercept in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py::main` dispatching to
  `rebar._cli._audit_commands.audit_cli` (story E landed `audit show` + `help/audit.txt`, and `audit`
  is already a known subcommand). This story **adds a `serve` subcommand** to the existing
  `audit_cli` dispatcher (which currently only handles `show`) and **updates `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt`**
  to document `serve`. Do NOT re-register the intercept or touch `known_subcommands()` — both already
  exist. `serve` parses `--host` (default `127.0.0.1`), `--port` (default `8765`), and lazily imports
  `rebar.audit.server`.

## Approach rationale

Reuse the reviewbot FastAPI/uvicorn stack rather than adding Flask or a bespoke `http.server`, so the
repo carries one web framework; lazy-import it inside the command (never at package init) so
`import rebar` stays dependency-free — the pattern the reviewbot extra already proves. `audit` already
owns a nested command grammar (`show`) and its own help text; this story extends that grammar with
`serve`. Ship a thin vertical slice (server + one real index page) so the risky packaging/gating/binding
path is exercised end-to-end from the first story.

## Compatibility & rollback

Default-off and behind an extra; `import rebar` unchanged. The `[ui]` section is additive (absent
`[ui]` defaults to `enabled=False`). Rollback = flag off / extra not installed. No data/schema change.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `[ui] enabled` round-trips through config via `rebar.toml`, `REBAR_UI_ENABLED`, and
      `-c ui.enabled=...`, each yielding `Config.ui.enabled == True`, and is NOT reported as an unknown
      key. Oracle: a unit test loading config through all three paths and asserting the value + absence
      of the "unknown key ignored" warning.
- [ ] With `[ui] enabled = false` (default), `import rebar` imports no web dependency and
      `rebar audit serve` refuses to start with a message that **names the `[ui] enabled` flag** and
      exits non-zero. Oracle: a test asserting no fastapi module is imported by `import rebar`, and that
      the disabled `audit serve` path returns non-zero AND its stderr message contains the flag name
      (`ui.enabled` / `[ui] enabled`) and starts no server.
- [ ] With the `ui` extra absent, `rebar audit serve` (when enabled) exits with an actionable "install
      nava-rebar[ui]" message, not a traceback. Oracle: a unit test simulating `ModuleNotFoundError`
      on the lazy `rebar.audit.server` import and asserting the message names the extra + non-zero exit.
- [ ] `rebar audit serve --host 0.0.0.0` prints a non-loopback exposure warning to stderr before
      binding; `--host 127.0.0.1` (default) does not. Oracle: a CLI test asserting the warning's
      presence/absence per host (without actually serving on a public interface).
- [ ] `rebar audit serve --port <n>` binds the given port (default `8765`). Oracle: the live
      end-to-end test binds an ephemeral `--port` and reaches the index there.
- [ ] With the feature enabled, the server binds 127.0.0.1 and serves an index page listing tickets
      that have audit data. Oracle: a live end-to-end test starting the server on an ephemeral port and
      asserting the index returns HTTP 200 and lists a seeded audited ticket (test-design §3
      server/CLI).
- [ ] `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass.
